mnemonics.title: SUB vs TRANS
mnemonics.content: SUBstitution changes WHAT letters are (replaces them). TRANSposition changes WHERE letters are (rearranges them). Sub = identity change, Trans = position change.

mnemonics.title: MONO vs POLY
mnemonics.content: MONOalphabetic = ONE alphabet (Caesar). POLYalphabetic = MANY alphabets (Vigenere). Mono preserves frequency = easy to break. Poly flattens frequency = harder to break.

mnemonics.title: Confusion + Diffusion = Shannon's Duo
mnemonics.content: Confusion: Substitution makes key-ciphertext relationship complex. Diffusion: Transposition spreads plaintext patterns across ciphertext. Strong ciphers need BOTH. Product ciphers achieve both through multiple rounds.

mnemonics.title: ETAOIN SHRDLU
mnemonics.content: English letter frequency order: E-T-A-O-I-N-S-H-R-D-L-U. This sequence is the key to frequency analysis. The most common ciphertext letter likely represents E (~12%), then T (~9%), then A (~8%).

mnemonics.title: Classical Cipher Family Tree
mnemonics.content: Classical ciphers branch into: Substitution (Caesar, Monoalphabetic, Vigenere, Playfair, Hill) and Transposition (Rail Fence, Columnar). Product ciphers combine both branches. All are insecure today.

keyPoints: Classical ciphers are divided into substitution (replace letters) and transposition (rearrange letters).
Substitution ciphers include monoalphabetic (one alphabet) and polyalphabetic (multiple alphabets).
Transposition ciphers rearrange letter positions without changing the letters themselves.
Shannon's confusion (from substitution) and diffusion (from transposition) are essential for strong ciphers.
Product ciphers combine substitution and transposition in multiple rounds (DES, AES).
All classical ciphers are insecure by modern standards but form the foundation for understanding modern cryptography.
commonMistakes: Confusing substitution (changes letter identity) with transposition (changes letter position).
Assuming classical ciphers provide adequate security for modern applications.
Mixing up confusion (substitution, key-ciphertext complexity) with diffusion (transposition, spreading plaintext statistics).
Forgetting that product ciphers combine BOTH substitution and transposition, not just one.
